skill.name: binary-re-triage
skill.description: Triage unknown binaries, ELF files, executables, and firmware blobs with fast architecture, ABI, dependency, and string checks.
skill.summary: Fast first-pass binary triage for reverse engineering and firmware analysis.
skill.author: 2389-research

security_audit.summary: The static command findings are mostly true in form but low-risk in context: the skill documents local file, rabin2, readelf, jq, and grep usage for binary triage. No malicious intent, prompt injection, credential exfiltration, or real network activity was found. Risk remains medium because users may parse untrusted binaries with external tools.

medium_findings.title: Local External Tool Execution for Untrusted Binary Triage
medium_findings.description: The skill instructs users to run file, rabin2, readelf, jq, and grep against unknown binaries. This is expected for the skill, but parsers can be exposed to malicious inputs.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command examples are clearly present and semantically tied to binary triage. Risk is limited because they analyze files rather than execute target binaries, and no shell interpolation is shown.

low_findings.title: Sensitive Strings May Be Surfaced in Triage Output
low_findings.description: The string scan explicitly searches for paths, passwords, keys, and tokens. This can reveal embedded secrets in reports or journals if users do not redact outputs.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The filter terms include pass, key, and token, and the skill asks users to record triage results. This is a data handling concern, not evidence of exfiltration.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Example URL Is Not Network Access
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URL appears only inside an example output object. The skill does not instruct the agent to fetch, post, or contact that URL.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URL is embedded in a sample strings_of_interest result. No network client command or data transmission path is present around this line.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Filesystem and Memory Terms Are Contextual
low_findings.description: The /dev/null use is output redirection, and mmap or mprotect are import names in a capability table. These are not active memory-mapped file operations.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line 65 suppresses readelf stderr, and line 149 is a static capability mapping row. Neither location implements file mapping or memory protection changes.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Weak Cryptography Was Not Found
low_findings.description: The flagged description line discusses binary fingerprinting with rabin2. No weak cryptographic algorithm or cryptographic implementation appears in the reviewed file.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The line contains skill metadata about architecture detection and file analysis. I found no DES, MD5, SHA1, RC4, or similar weak crypto usage there.

dangerous_patterns.title: Shell Commands in Documentation
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill contains bash command blocks for local binary triage. These commands should be run in an isolated workspace when the binary is untrusted.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: Multiple command blocks are present and intended for use. They are legitimate but still expose local analysis tools to malformed binaries.

content.value_statement: Unknown binaries slow reverse engineering because basic facts are missing. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through fast first-pass identification.
actual_capabilities: Identifies architecture, bit width, endianness, link type, and interpreter data.
Uses rabin2 and readelf examples to collect structured metadata.
Maps interpreter paths to common libc families such as glibc, musl, and uClibc.
Reviews imports, exports, dependencies, and strings for capability clues.
Produces a concise triage record with facts, hypotheses, questions, and next steps.
limitations: It does not perform deep decompilation or control-flow analysis.
It does not safely execute unknown binaries or observe runtime behavior.
It depends on local tools such as rabin2, readelf, jq, grep, and file.
It can surface sensitive strings that users must redact before sharing.

use_cases.title: Firmware Intake
use_cases.description: Identify CPU architecture, libc, dependencies, and likely capabilities before selecting a firmware analysis path.

use_cases.title: Malware Sample Scoping
use_cases.description: Collect non-execution facts from an unknown executable before deeper static or dynamic analysis.

use_cases.title: Toolchain Selection
use_cases.description: Use ABI, interpreter, and architecture details to choose Ghidra, radare2, QEMU, or a sysroot.

prompt_templates.title: Identify This Binary
prompt_templates.prompt: Triage this unknown binary. Report architecture, bit width, endianness, link type, interpreter, and immediate next steps.
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner triage for a single executable.

prompt_templates.title: Summarize ELF Metadata
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the binary triage workflow to summarize ELF metadata, dependencies, imports, exports, and strings of interest.

prompt_templates.title: Infer Capabilities
prompt_templates.prompt: Analyze imports, libraries, and strings from this binary. Infer likely capabilities and state confidence for each hypothesis.

prompt_templates.title: Plan Next Analysis Phase
prompt_templates.prompt: Triage this firmware binary and recommend the next static or dynamic analysis phase. Include sysroot and tooling requirements.
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced planning for a reverse-engineering workflow.

output_examples.input: An unknown ARM ELF from an embedded device needs first-pass review.
output: Capabilities: likely network client and TLS support based on libraries and imports.
Next step: static analysis with a matching ARM musl sysroot.

output_examples.input: A stripped executable contains many URLs and configuration paths.
output: The binary appears stripped, so symbol recovery may be limited.
Strings suggest configuration file access and outbound HTTP communication.
Review sensitive strings before sharing the triage notes.

output_examples.input: A shared library needs quick dependency and export review.
output: Dependencies show compression and threading support.
Next step: enumerate functions and cross-references in a static analysis tool.

best_practices: Run triage tools in an isolated workspace when samples are untrusted.
Record facts separately from hypotheses and include evidence for each claim.
Redact tokens, keys, credentials, and internal paths before sharing output.
anti_patterns: Do not execute an unknown binary during first-pass triage.
Do not treat import names alone as proof of behavior.
Do not publish raw strings that may contain secrets or customer data.

faq.question: Does this skill execute unknown binaries?
faq.answer: No. It focuses on static fingerprinting with local analysis tools.

faq.question: Which tools does it expect?
faq.answer: It references file, rabin2, readelf, jq, and grep.

faq.question: Can it identify firmware architecture?
faq.answer: Yes. It guides architecture, bit width, endianness, ABI, and libc identification.

faq.question: Does it replace Ghidra or radare2 analysis?
faq.answer: No. It prepares facts for deeper tools and helps choose the next phase.

faq.question: Is it safe for malware samples?
faq.answer: Use isolation. The workflow avoids execution but still parses untrusted files.

faq.question: What should be redacted from output?
faq.answer: Remove tokens, keys, passwords, customer paths, hostnames, and sensitive URLs.
